definition: The fundamental economic problem that arises because human wants are unlimited, but the resources available to satisfy those wants are limited.
example: A country has a finite supply of oil but unlimited demand for energy.

definition: The value of the next best alternative forgone when making an economic choice.
example: The opportunity cost of going to college full-time is the wage income forgone.

definition: A curve showing all the maximum combinations of two goods or services an economy can produce when all resources are fully and efficiently employed.
example: A PPF showing combinations of consumer goods and capital goods an economy can produce.

definition: Statements about the economy that are objective, factual, and can be tested against evidence.
example: 'Unemployment rose by 2% last year' — this is a positive statement that can be verified.

definition: Statements about the economy that are based on value judgements and opinions about what ought to be, and cannot be tested against evidence.
example: 'The government should raise the minimum wage' — this is a normative statement.

definition: The breaking down of the production process into a series of separate tasks, each carried out by a specialist worker.
example: In a car factory, one worker fits doors while another installs the engine.

definition: The resources used in the production of goods and services: Land, Labour, Capital, and Enterprise.
example: A bakery uses land (premises), labour (bakers), capital (ovens), and enterprise (the owner).

definition: As the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded falls, ceteris paribus (all other things being equal).
example: When the price of coffee rises from €2 to €3, consumers buy fewer cups per day.

definition: As the price of a good rises, the quantity supplied increases, ceteris paribus.
example: When the price of wheat rises, farmers plant more wheat to increase profit.

definition: The point where the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied, resulting in a stable market price with no tendency to change.
example: If the market price of apples is €1.50/kg, and supply equals demand at that price, equilibrium exists.

definition: The difference between the maximum price a consumer is willing to pay for a good and the price they actually pay.
example: A consumer willing to pay €10 for a book but pays €6 has a consumer surplus of €4.

definition: The difference between the price a producer receives for a good and the minimum price they would have accepted.
example: A farmer willing to sell grain for €2/kg but receives €3/kg has a producer surplus of €1/kg.

definition: A measure of the responsiveness of the quantity demanded of a good to a change in its price. PED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price.
example: If a 10% price rise causes a 20% fall in demand, PED = −2 (elastic demand).

definition: Demand is elastic when a percentage change in price causes a greater percentage change in quantity demanded (PED > 1). Demand is sensitive to price changes.
example: Luxury goods, goods with many substitutes, e.g. a specific brand of crisps.

definition: Demand is inelastic when a percentage change in price causes a smaller percentage change in quantity demanded (PED < 1). Demand is relatively insensitive to price changes.
example: Necessities like petrol or insulin — consumers buy roughly the same amount regardless of price.

definition: A measure of the responsiveness of demand to a change in consumer income. YED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in income.
example: If income rises 10% and demand for foreign holidays rises 30%, YED = +3 (luxury good).

definition: A measure of the responsiveness of demand for one good to a change in the price of another good. XED = % change in quantity demanded of Good A ÷ % change in price of Good B.
example: If the price of butter rises and demand for margarine rises, XED is positive (substitutes).

definition: A good for which demand increases as consumer income rises (positive YED).
example: Restaurants, new cars, foreign holidays.

definition: A good for which demand decreases as consumer income rises (negative YED), as consumers switch to higher-quality alternatives.
example: Own-brand supermarket goods, second-hand clothes.

definition: An inferior good for which demand increases as price rises, violating the law of demand. The negative income effect outweighs the substitution effect.
example: Historically, staple foods like bread or rice among very poor households.

definition: Goods that can replace one another in consumption. A rise in the price of one leads to an increase in demand for the other (positive XED).
example: Tea and coffee, butter and margarine.

definition: Goods that are consumed together. A rise in the price of one leads to a fall in demand for the other (negative XED).
example: Cars and petrol, printers and ink cartridges.

definition: The additional satisfaction or utility gained by a consumer from consuming one extra unit of a good or service.
example: The 1st cup of water on a hot day provides high marginal utility; the 10th provides very little.

definition: As a consumer consumes successive units of a good, the marginal utility (extra satisfaction) gained from each additional unit decreases.
example: Each additional slice of pizza eaten gives less satisfaction than the slice before.

definition: A consumer maximises utility by allocating income so that the marginal utility per euro spent is equal for all goods purchased: MU₁/P₁ = MU₂/P₂.
example: A consumer in equilibrium when the last euro spent on tea gives the same utility as the last euro on coffee.

definition: In the short run, as more units of a variable factor (e.g. labour) are added to a fixed factor, marginal product will eventually decrease.
example: Adding more chefs to a restaurant with a fixed kitchen eventually produces less extra output per chef.

definition: The cost advantages a firm gains as it increases its scale of production, resulting in a fall in long-run average costs.
example: Bulk buying discounts, specialisation of labour, spreading fixed costs over more output.

definition: The disadvantages of large-scale production that cause long-run average costs to rise as output increases beyond an optimal level.
example: Communication problems, poor worker motivation, and managerial inefficiencies in very large firms.

definition: A market structure with many buyers and sellers, a homogeneous product, freedom of entry/exit, and perfect information. Firms are price takers.
example: The market for agricultural commodities like wheat or potatoes approximates perfect competition.

definition: A market structure in which a single firm is the sole producer of a good or service with no close substitutes and significant barriers to entry.
example: Irish Water as the sole provider of water services in Ireland.

definition: A market structure dominated by a small number of large, interdependent firms producing similar or differentiated products, with significant barriers to entry.
example: The Irish banking sector, dominated by a few large banks (AIB, Bank of Ireland, PTSB).

definition: A measure of the market share held by the largest firms in an industry. A high concentration ratio indicates an oligopolistic or monopolistic market.
example: If the top 3 supermarkets hold 80% of the market, the 3-firm concentration ratio is 80%.

definition: When a firm charges different prices to different consumers for the same good or service, not based on cost differences.
example: Airlines charging higher prices to business travellers who book last minute than leisure travellers who book early.

definition: An agreement (formal or informal) between competing firms to fix prices, output levels, or market share in order to reduce competition and increase profits.
example: A cartel of oil producers agreeing to restrict supply to keep oil prices high.

definition: A market where the minimum efficient scale of production is so large that only one firm can profitably operate; average costs fall continuously as output increases.
example: Rail infrastructure, electricity grid — it would be wasteful to have multiple competing networks.

definition: A situation where the free market fails to allocate resources efficiently, leading to an under- or over-provision of goods and services.
example: Pollution — the free market overproduces goods that generate negative externalities.

definition: A cost or benefit that falls on a third party (not the buyer or seller) as a result of an economic transaction.
example: Negative: factory pollution affecting local residents. Positive: vaccination protecting the wider community.

definition: A cost imposed on a third party who is not involved in the transaction. Leads to overproduction relative to the socially optimal level.
example: Carbon emissions from cars causing air pollution and health costs to society.

definition: A benefit enjoyed by a third party who is not involved in the transaction. Leads to underproduction relative to the socially optimal level.
example: Education benefits the wider economy through a more productive workforce.

definition: A good that is non-excludable (cannot prevent non-payers from consuming it) and non-rival (one person's use does not reduce availability to others).
example: National defence, street lighting, public fireworks displays.

definition: A good that is underprovided by the free market because its full social benefits are not recognised by consumers. The government encourages consumption.
example: Education, healthcare, vaccination programmes.

definition: A good that is overprovided by the free market because its full social costs are not recognised by consumers. The government discourages consumption.
example: Cigarettes, alcohol, illegal drugs.

definition: The total monetary value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given time period.
example: Ireland's GDP includes the output of all firms operating in Ireland, including foreign multinationals.

definition: The total monetary value of all final goods and services produced by a country's residents (nationals) in a given time period, regardless of location. GNP = GDP + Net Factor Income from Abroad.
example: Ireland's GNP excludes profits repatriated by foreign multinationals operating here.

definition: A modified measure of national income for Ireland that adjusts GNP to remove the distorting effects of globalisation (e.g. aircraft leasing, IP transfers by multinationals).
example: Ireland's GNI* is significantly lower than GDP due to large multinational profits recorded here.

definition: A model showing the flow of income, output, and expenditure between households and firms in an economy, with injections (I, G, X) and leakages (S, T, M).
example: Firms pay wages to households; households spend on goods; firms receive revenue — the flow continues.

definition: The process by which an initial injection of spending into the economy leads to a larger final increase in national income. Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 − MPC) or 1 ÷ MPS.
example: A €100m government infrastructure spend creates more than €100m of additional income as money circulates.

definition: The proportion of additional income that is spent on consumption. MPC = Change in consumption ÷ Change in income.
example: If income rises by €100 and consumption rises by €80, MPC = 0.8.

definition: The proportion of additional income that is saved. MPS = Change in savings ÷ Change in income. MPC + MPS = 1.
example: If income rises by €100 and savings rise by €20, MPS = 0.2.

definition: The use of government taxation and expenditure to manage the level of economic activity and achieve macroeconomic objectives.
example: Cutting income tax to stimulate consumer spending during a recession (expansionary fiscal policy).

definition: A government policy of increasing spending and/or cutting taxes to stimulate aggregate demand and boost economic growth.
example: Used during the 2008 recession — governments increased spending on public works to create jobs.

definition: A government policy of decreasing spending and/or raising taxes to reduce aggregate demand and control inflation.
example: Ireland's austerity measures post-2008 — tax hikes and spending cuts to reduce the deficit.

definition: When government expenditure exceeds government revenue in a given year.
example: Ireland ran significant budget deficits after the 2008 financial crisis.

definition: When government revenue exceeds government expenditure in a given year.
example: Ireland recorded budget surpluses in the Celtic Tiger years and again from 2023.

definition: The total accumulated amount of money owed by the government to domestic and foreign creditors, built up from past budget deficits.
example: Ireland's national debt rose sharply after the banking crisis of 2008.

definition: Elements of fiscal policy that automatically act to reduce fluctuations in economic activity without deliberate government action, e.g. unemployment benefits and progressive taxation.
example: In a recession, tax revenues fall and social welfare payments rise — cushioning the fall in demand automatically.

definition: A tax where the proportion of income paid in tax increases as income rises. Higher earners pay a higher percentage of their income in tax.
example: Ireland's income tax system — lower earners pay 20%; higher earners pay 40%.

definition: A tax where the proportion of income paid in tax decreases as income rises. Lower earners pay a higher percentage of their income in tax.
example: VAT — a 23% VAT on goods takes a larger share of income from low earners than high earners.

definition: A tax where every taxpayer pays the same percentage of their income, regardless of earnings.
example: A flat 20% income tax applied to all income earners equally.

definition: The management of the money supply and interest rates by a central bank (e.g. the ECB) to achieve macroeconomic objectives such as price stability.
example: The ECB raising interest rates in 2022–2023 to reduce inflation across the Eurozone.

definition: The central bank of the Eurozone, responsible for maintaining price stability (inflation close to but below 2%) and conducting monetary policy for all Eurozone members.
example: The ECB sets the key interest rates for the 20 Eurozone countries, including Ireland.

definition: A sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy over a period of time, resulting in a fall in the purchasing power of money.
example: If inflation is 5%, a basket of goods costing €100 today will cost €105 next year.

definition: A measure of the average change in prices paid by consumers for a representative basket of goods and services over time. Used to measure inflation.
example: The CSO calculates the Irish CPI monthly by tracking the prices of a fixed basket of goods and services.

definition: Inflation caused by an increase in aggregate demand in the economy, where demand outstrips the productive capacity of the economy.
example: During the Celtic Tiger, strong economic growth and rising consumer spending pushed prices upward.

definition: Inflation caused by an increase in the costs of production (e.g. wages, raw materials), which firms pass on as higher prices.
example: The 1973 oil crisis caused cost-push inflation globally as energy costs surged.

definition: The situation where people who are willing and able to work at the current wage rate cannot find employment.
example: Measured in Ireland by the Live Register and the Quarterly National Household Survey (QNHS).

definition: Temporary unemployment that occurs when workers are between jobs and searching for new employment. It is short-term and inevitable.
example: A worker who has resigned from one job but has not yet started their next role.

definition: Long-term unemployment caused by a permanent decline in demand for workers in a particular industry due to technological change or shifts in consumer demand.
example: Coal miners becoming unemployed as coal power plants close due to the shift to renewables.

definition: Unemployment caused by a fall in aggregate demand during an economic recession or downturn. Also called demand-deficient unemployment.
example: Mass redundancies during the 2008–2012 recession in Ireland as businesses cut costs.

definition: Unemployment that occurs at certain times of the year due to the seasonal nature of some industries.
example: Tourism workers laid off during winter months in coastal towns.

definition: Demand for a factor of production (e.g. labour) that arises indirectly from the demand for the goods or services it produces.
example: Demand for electricians rises because demand for new houses is rising.

definition: The payment made to a factor of production over and above its transfer earnings (the minimum needed to keep it in its current use).
example: A top footballer earning €5m/year when they would accept €50,000 earns economic rent of €4.95m.

definition: A country has an absolute advantage in producing a good if it can produce more of it using the same amount of resources than another country.
example: Ireland has an absolute advantage in dairy production compared to desert countries.

definition: A country has a comparative advantage in producing a good if it can produce it at a lower opportunity cost than another country.
example: Even if Ireland is more efficient at producing both beef and technology, it should specialise in whichever has the lower opportunity cost.

definition: A record of all economic transactions between residents of a country and the rest of the world over a period of time. It comprises the Current Account and the Capital & Financial Account.
example: Ireland's balance of payments records exports, imports, investment flows, and transfers.

definition: Part of the Balance of Payments that records trade in goods (visible trade), trade in services (invisible trade), income flows, and current transfers.
example: Ireland typically has a current account surplus due to high exports of pharmaceutical and tech services.

definition: When the value of a country's imports of goods and services exceeds the value of its exports over a given period.
example: The UK often runs a trade deficit — it imports more goods than it exports.

definition: A tax imposed on imported goods to raise their price, making domestic goods more competitive and protecting domestic industries.
example: A 25% tariff on imported cars raises their price, encouraging consumers to buy domestically produced cars.

definition: A physical limit on the quantity of a particular good that can be imported into a country over a given period.
example: A quota of 10,000 cars per year from Japan restricts the number that can be imported.

definition: Investment by a company or individual in one country in business interests located in another country, usually involving establishing business operations or acquiring business assets.
example: Apple, Google, and Meta establishing their European headquarters in Ireland.

definition: A composite statistic used to rank countries by level of human development, combining three dimensions: life expectancy, education, and per capita income.
example: Norway consistently ranks highest on the HDI; many Sub-Saharan African nations rank lowest.

definition: The price at which one country's currency can be exchanged for another's. Determined by supply and demand in the foreign exchange market.
example: If €1 = $1.10, the exchange rate of the euro against the dollar is 1.10.

definition: A fall in the value of a country's currency relative to other currencies in a floating exchange rate system.
example: If €1 falls from $1.20 to $1.05, the euro has depreciated — Irish exports become cheaper abroad.

definition: A minimum price set by the government above the equilibrium price, preventing the market price from falling below it. Creates a surplus.
example: The EU Common Agricultural Policy sets minimum prices for agricultural products.

definition: A maximum price set by the government below the equilibrium price, preventing the market price from rising above it. Creates a shortage.
example: Rent controls in cities set a maximum monthly rent to keep housing affordable.
